name: Monster Patrol
description: A roaming threat behavior where a monster follows routes, reacts to perception events, investigates suspicious locations, and escalates into search or chase states.
design_notes: Monster patrols build dread when players can learn patterns but never feel fully safe. The system should expose route ids, alert state, last known player position, perception events, search timeout, path failures, and debug reason traces. Designers need tools to see why the monster moved, heard, searched, or gave up.
design_purpose: Create persistent threat pressure across rooms and corridors.
Connect noise, visibility, hiding, safe rooms, and chase behavior into one AI loop.
Give level designers predictable patrol tools with controlled uncertainty.
player_fantasy: The player feels hunted by something that patrols the space, reacts to mistakes, and may return when they least want it.

parameters.name: monster_id
parameters.description: Threat instance or archetype running patrol behavior.

parameters.name: monster_patrol_route_id
parameters.description: Route definition used while the monster is not fully alerted.

parameters.name: monster_alert_state
parameters.description: Current AI state used by animation, audio, and decisions.

parameters.name: last_known_player_position
parameters.description: Most recent suspected player location from sight, sound, or script.

parameters.name: perception_event_id
parameters.description: Stimulus that caused the latest state transition.

parameters.name: search_timeout_ms
parameters.description: Search window before returning to patrol or escalating elsewhere.

parameters.name: debug_reason_trace
parameters.description: Designer-readable AI decision history for tuning and bug reports.

edge_cases: The player enters a safe room while the monster is already chasing at the boundary.
A noise lure and line-of-sight event arrive during the same AI decision step.
Navmesh updates or locked doors invalidate the current patrol route.
The monster loses sight of the player while they enter a hiding spot.
Checkpoint restore spawns the monster with stale last_known_player_position near the player.
common_bugs: Patrol state changes are driven by animation not AI authority, causing desync between movement and decisions.
Search timeout resets every frame because suspicion never decays below the transition threshold.
The monster pathfinds to the exact player position after hearing a lure instead of the noise source.
Safe-room exclusion blocks movement but not attack traces or audio stingers.
Debug logs show the chosen route but not the perception event that caused the switch.
balancing_notes: Predictable patrols support planning; occasional route variation supports fear when it has readable rules.
Search behavior should punish noisy mistakes without turning every small sound into a guaranteed chase.
Safe-room boundary behavior defines trust; inconsistent exceptions make players stop learning the system.
accessibility_notes: Provide subtitle, caption, or visual alternatives for monster audio cues.
Offer difficulty or assist settings that adjust search persistence and chase recovery without disabling threat identity.
Avoid requiring players to distinguish extremely subtle audio direction under high stress.

implementation_notes.unity: Use NavMeshAgent or a custom controller with an explicit monster_alert_state and perception_event_id queue. Store last_known_player_position, route index, and debug_reason_trace so designers can inspect why patrol, search, or chase transitions occurred.
implementation_notes.godot: Use NavigationAgent2D or NavigationAgent3D with a state machine for patrol, suspicion, search, chase, and return. Route perception signals through one decision method so noise, sight, and safe-room boundaries resolve in deterministic priority order.
implementation_notes.unreal: Behavior Trees and Blackboard keys can store monster_patrol_route_id, last_known_player_position, and alert state. Use decorators or services for line-of-sight and noise, and log abort reasons so designers can debug unexpected search loops.
implementation_notes.web: Represent patrols as data graphs and update AI on fixed decision intervals. Phaser or custom pathfinding should store route node, alert state, and last known position in save or replay state so reports are reproducible.

example_games.title: Alien: Isolation
example_games.note: Uses a roaming alien threat that searches and reacts to player behavior.

example_games.title: Amnesia: The Bunker
example_games.note: Uses a persistent monster threat connected to sound and exploration pressure.
